text:
Admission Date:  1996-06-07              Discharge Date:   2000-06-03

Date of Birth:  1987-01-19             Sex:   M

Service: CARDIOTHORACIC

Allergies:
Codeine / Penicillins

Attending:Willie
Chief Complaint:
Chest Pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1996-06-07 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft x 5 (Left internal mammary
artery to left anterior descending, Saphenous vein graft to 1st
Diagonal, Saphenous vein graft to 2nd Diagonal, Saphenous vein
graft to Posterior descending artery, Saphenous vein graft to
Posterior lateral branch)

History of Present Illness:
This 57 year old man with no cardiac history but history of
hyperlipidemia and strong family history presented to outside
hospital with sudden onset epigastric pain and chest tightness
started at 4:30am. On presentation he was noted to have inferior
ST elevation on EKG and was tranfered to Bon Secours Mary Immaculate Hospital for cardiac
catheterization.

Past Medical History:
Hypercholesterolemia
Diverticulitis

Social History:
Denies tobacco or ETOH use.

Family History:
Brother with Coronary disease s/p coronary artery bypass surgery

Physical Exam:
VS: 80 22 118/84 5'[**54**]" 200#
Gen: WDWN male in NAD lying flat on cath lab table
Skin: Unremarkable
HEENT: Unremarkable
Neck: Supple, fullrange of motion
Chest: Clear bilaterally
Heart: Regular rate and rhythm without murmur
Abd: Soft non-tender, non-distended
Ext: Warm, well-perfused, -edema, palpable pulses
Neuro: A&O x 3, non-focal

Pertinent Results:
1996-06-07 Cardiac Cath: 1. Selective coronary angiography of this
right dominant system demonstrated 2 vessel coronary artery
disease. The LMCA had 30% origin and 30% distal disease. The LAD
had 40% origin, 90% mid and 70% distal disease. The LCX was a
small vessel. The RCA had 40% proximal stenosis, 90% mid with
extensive thrombus, 90% distal and 90% PDA stenoses. 2. Resting
hemodynamics were performed. The right sided filling pressures
were mildly elevated (mean RA pressure was 11mmHg and RVEDP was
12mmHg). The left sided filling pressures were elevated (mean
PCW pressure was 17mmHg). The pulmonary arteyr pressures were
within normal range. The systemic arterial pressures were within
normal range. The cardiac index measured using an assumed oxygen
consumption rate was normal at 2.7 L/Min/M2. 3. Successful
placement of balloon pump through the right common femoral
artery.
01-29 Echo: PRE-BYPASS: No atrial septal defect is seen by 2D or
color Doppler. Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and
global systolic function are normal (LVEF>55%). There is
inferior wall hypokinesis. Right ventricular chamber size and
free wall motion are normal. The ascending and descending
thoracic aorta are normal in diameter. There are simple atheroma
in the aortic arch. The aortic valve leaflets (3) appear
structurally normal with good leaflet excursion and no aortic
stenosis. The mitral valve appears structurally normal with
trivial mitral regurgitation. There is no pericardial effusion.
The IABP is in good position 06-16 centimeters distal to the
origen of the left subclavian artery. POST-BYPASS: Left and
right ventricular function is preserved. The aorta is intact.
Inferior wall hypokinesis persists. The remainder of the
examination is unchanged.

Brief Hospital Course:
As mentioned in the HPI, Mr. Cook was transferred to Bon Secours Mary Immaculate Hospital
with chest pain and ST elevation on EKG. He was brought directly
to the cardiac cath lab. Cath revealed severe 2 vessel coronary
disease with right coronary artery thrombus. An IABP was placed
in cath lab and he was brought to the operating room for
emergent surgery. He underwent a coronary artery bypass graft x
5. Please see operative report for surgical details. Following
surgery he was transferred to the CVICU for invasive monitoring
in stable condition. Within 24 hours he was weaned from
sedation, awoke neurologically intact and extubated. On post-op
day one the IABP was removed. He was started on beta blockers
and diuretics and gently diuresed towards his pre-op weight. On
post-op day two he was transferred to the telemetry floor for
further care. Chest tubes and epicardial pacing wires were
removed per protocol.  Physical therapy was consulted to work on
strength and balance. He continued to progress and was cleared
for discharge on POD 5.

Medications on Admission:
Home: Zocor
In hosp: Aspirin 325mg qd, Plavix 600mg 01-29, Lovenox,
Aggrastat, Integrilin, NTG SL

Discharge Medications:
1. Aspirin 81 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One (1)
Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.)(s)* Refills:*2*

times a day).
Disp:*60 Capsule(s)* Refills:*2*
3. Oxycodone-Acetaminophen 5-325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO
Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed for pain.
Disp:*40 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*

Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*1*
5. Lisinopril 5 mg Tablet Sig: 0.5 Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
6. Zocor 80 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO QHS.
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
7. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: Three (3) Tablet PO BID
(2 times a day).
Disp:*180 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
8. Furosemide 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q12H (every 12
hours) for 2 weeks.
Disp:*28 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
9. Potassium Chloride 20 mEq Tab Sust.Rel. Particle/Crystal Sig:
One (1) Tab Sust.Rel. Particle/Crystal PO Q12H (every 12 hours)
for 2 weeks.
Disp:*28 Tab Sust.Rel. Particle/Crystal(s)* Refills:*0*
10. Ranitidine HCl 150 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
Diversified VNA and hospice

Discharge Diagnosis:
Coronary Artery Disease s/p Coronary Artery Bypass Graft x 5
Myocaridal Infarction
PMH: Hypercholesterolemia, Diverticulitis

Discharge Condition:
Good

Discharge Instructions:
1)  Monitor wounds for signs of infection. These include
redness, drainage or increased pain. In the event that you have
drainage from your sternal wound, please contact the [**Name2 (NI) 5059**] at
(620-527-4989.
2)  Report any fever greater then 100.5.
3)  Report any weight gain of 2 pounds in 24 hours or 5 pounds
in 1 week.
4)  No lotions, creams or powders to incision until it has
healed. You may shower and wash incision. Gently pat the wound
dry.  Please shower daily.  No bathing or swimming for 1 month.
Use sunscreen on incision if exposed to sun.
5) No lifting greater then 10 pounds for 10 weeks.
6) No driving for 1 month or while taking narcotics for pain.
7) Call with any questions or concerns.

Followup Instructions:
Dr. Brian in 4 weeks
Dr. Lloyd in 06-16 weeks
Jacqueline Smith Kaylee Thomas, M.D. 1-2 weeks (06641 Walter Row
Port Janice, RI 92215,Suite #150,
84098 Weaver Vista
